[IMAGE:0]It's a little after midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Victoria Woodhull, 19th century occultist, alternative healer, spiritualist, feminist, and the first woman to run for president of the US, in 1872 -- no, women didn't have the vote yet, but that didn't stop her from trying. She and her sister Tennessee Claflin also ran the first women-owned brokerage firm on Wall Street and published a newspaper. Yes, the history of American occultism is full of colorful figures like her... 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

***Ahem. I just had someone try to put through a brand new question an hour after I marked this closed. The question was deleted. I do have to put time into other things, you know! "See you next week" means please save your questions until then...***

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Thank you for Magic Monday!

I have four unrelated questions:

1) I did the exercise in lesson 5 of the Order of Essenes, and found the ring moved as I desired. What is happening there?

2) I had an odd thought about US politics, and I’d like to know if it sounds plausible to you. It’s based on an observation: lots of Democrats and Republicans seem more interested in fighting the other party than anything else, and the words those parties are named after have meanings. “Democracy”, as used in the US, typically refers to the ability of everyone to make their voice heard, and “republic”, in the US system, typically refers to the Constituional Republic, and so it’s a rules based system.

What I find striking is that so many Republicans seem to go out of their way to find clever and creative ways to exclude people, and meanwhile the Democrats seem happy to devise all sorts of clever ways to change the rules, and have recently descended into what looks like mob rule: in other words, what “democarcy” without being part of a “republic” looks like.

What I wonder is if this is occuring because the parties are focused on fighting each other, and so they’re rejecting the other, and thus at some deep level rejecting the idea which the other party was named for. Does this make sense, and do things like this happen?

3) I’ve recently taken some whole systems thinking to my internet use: the problem was that it was too easy to access it. I fixed that by deleting my web-browser and forcing myself to reinstall it before any use. This cut about a third of my use right away, but also, about half the time when I go to use the internet I find myself deciding it’s not worth while between when I start to install the browser and when I get to use it.

Given I’ve seen various discussions of the need to keep a unified will, is there a risk here of dividing my will, and if so, how do I avoid it?

4) Magic Monday starts at midnight, meaning it starts before sunrise, the start of the astrological day, so is it astrologically Magic Sunday?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Wait and see -- it'll be discussed in a later lesson!

2) Of course things like that happen. The name you choose for a party, a project, or anything else can have a powerful influence on how it turns out, because the connotations of the name shape how you and others experience it.

3) That's a very straightforward use of will. Remember that most of the actions you do are habit rather than will, and so using a delaying tactic like that to short-circuit the habit and make sure you actually will to go on line is a nice trick.

4) Depends on where you are at the moment the post goes up. Here in East Providence, it was the hour of Saturn on the day of the Sun, but the Sun was already up over Europe at that moment...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
3) What about changing my mind? Is that part risky for dividing will, or is it safe to do so?

4) So your experience doesn't matter, but rather everyone who participates comes to it with a different astrological experience, based on where they are? My head hurts trying to make sense of that....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
3) Changing your mind weakens your will. Sometimes it's necessary -- when, for example, you realize that your decision was a mistake -- but there's always a cost. This is why it's important to assess all the issues, and if possible use divination as well, before directing your will.

4) The world is a complex place, and each of us sees it from a different angle -- part of that being the astrology. At this moment, similarly, the relationship of the planets to the ascendant, midheaven, and house cusps depends utterly on where you are -- what's at the midheaven here is at the nadir in east Asia...

comments.title: Visualisation
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

About a month ago I posted about the difficulty I have been having doing the exercise in Mouni Sadhu's Concentration where you have to visualise the pin.

I am still unable to do the visualisation. I have been experimenting with different variations on the exercise to try and make progress eg. trying to visualise basic shapes and colours such as a red square or a yellow circle. I've had no luck with these either. This is quite frustrating as I had been making solid progress with the exercises.

As I was reflecting on the exercise, I recalled that I am able to do other kinds of exercises that seem to imply visualisation. For example, the exercise in LRM where you ask the student to recall a familiar path from everyday life eg. walking to the local shop, and bring it to mind. I have no trouble with this exercise. Similarly, I have no problem with imagining made up scenarios.

So, I'm wondering, would you draw a distinction between these kinds of "visualisations" and Sadhu's pin exercise or are they the same thing? Does it make any difference whether one visualises from memory, from imagination, or from the mental plane directly?

On a slightly different note, when I do Sadhu's exercise I find myself trying to use my eyes to "see" the pin. Is this likely to be a problem? It seems from Sadhu's language that the end goal is to actually be able to see something as if you did see it with your own eyes. But obviously visualisation can't actually be using the eyes.

Grateful for any response you can give which might help me to get over this hurdle.

Simon.

replies.title: Re: Visualisation
replies.content: Some people have a much easier time with visualization than others, and some people can't visualize at all. Have you had better luck with the exercise of concentrating on sound?

replies.title: Re: Visualisation
replies.content:
Yes. I have become adept at both the visual and aural focus exercises and can regularly get to 10 minutes with both. In fact, I find the aural ones easier, which probably has something to do with the fact that I have spent hundreds of hours mixing recorded music (a hobby of mine over a number of years).

It sounds like I should probably just cut my losses with the visualisation and focus on other areas.

replies.title: Re: Visualisation
replies.content: That seems like a reasonable approach to me.

comments.title: CGD temperature tables
comments.content:
I am having some issues with the temperature tables in the CGD book.

1) Honey is mentioned as "warm & dry 2°". I am assuming it is "hot & dry 2°".

2) Here in Brazil there are two kinds of unprocessed sugar (both made from sugarcane): one that looks like small quartz crystals, and one that is a brown powder (Wikipedia tells me the name in English is brown sugar). I am assuming that "unprocessed" is the "crystalline" variety (here it is sold as opposed to refined sugar).

3) Camphor is mentioned in Herbs as "cold & moist 3°" and as "hot & dry 3°" in Other. Why?

4) Keep a diet only with the foods mentioned in the tables would be troublesome. Is there a place where expanded tables can be obtained?

replies.title: Re: CGD temperature tables
replies.content:
1) Yes, "hot" and "warm" are equivalent terms in the old temperature system.

2) Yes. Brown sugar is one degree moister.

3) The listing in "Herbs" is a typo.

4) If you can find books on the Unani or Unani Tibb system of healing, that's a version of the same thing which is still practiced in parts of the Middle East and in India. Other than that, how's your Latin? There's quite a bit on this subject that hasn't been translated ye out of medieval and Renaissance sources.

replies.title: Re: CGD temperature tables
replies.content:
Thank you. My Latin is, of course, nonexistent, but I'm willing to give it a try. Could I have some titles?

Disclaimer, I understand the risks of attempting to translate the names of edible items from unknown languages and I will be careful.

And while we are here, I only was able to catch the error in camphor because the reference was duplicated. Are there other known typos in the CGD temperature tables?

replies.title: Re: CGD temperature tables
replies.content: Avicenna's The Canon of Medicine and The Book of Healing would be good places to start looking. As for typos, I didn't know about that one until you mentioned it -- I'm familiar with camphor, and know that it's warming and drying.

replies.title: Re: CGD temperature tables
replies.content: Again, thank you very much.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Okay, the best place to start with Platonism is with Plato himself. At minimum, the Meno and the Republic will give you a basic sense of his approach. Then go on to John Dillon's The Middle Platonists, and then to any decent book on Plotinus -- I'm fond of Rist's Plotinus: The Road to Reality, but there are a lot of good surveys of his thought. Follow that up with some dippings into The Enneads themselves. Next, Iamblichus' On the Mysteries, and finally, Proclus' The Elements of Theology.

Hi, I was hoping you'd be kind enough to aid me in the interpretation of a geomantic chart that I recently cast. The question was 'would the result of letting out my property of the address:... be what I hope it would be'.

I got:
1 fortuna minor
2 tristitia
3 fortuna minor
4 cauda draconis
5 puella
6 puella
7 tristitia
8 rubeus
9 puer
10 albus
11 populus

RW via
judge amissio
LW acquisitio
Reconciler conjunctio

Am I right in taking the 4th house as the significator of the quested rather than the eighth? In which case there's a straight forward conjunction, and fortuna minor as my significator is positive, though cauda in the fourth one would think not. Letting out the property would involve me moving out, and I've lived there for quite a while now - I don't know if cauda could perhaps be a comment on this. Should I pay any attention to tristitia in the 7th house moving to the 2nd in conjunction with my significator and also aspecting the 4th?

Which brings me to the main thing that troubled me, the judge amissio. I usually only interpret the court secondarily to the house chart, but this judge at first to me seemed to me like a straight no. As certainly a large part of whether of whether the letting out would be satisfactory to me is if it works out financially.

I've come to think more that amissio perhaps speaks to letting go of the past - the time I've been living there (when I've had some rather difficult times), and an improval of health on leaving. My plan behind letting out has been to move town as well as property. Do you have any thoughts on this, am I reading into this just what I want to see?

The reconciler conjunctio, doesn't really illuminate the seeming contradiction between the house chart and judge to me either.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The house of the quesited is the fourth, because it deals with real estate -- I'm not sure why you thought it might be the eighth. (Did you inherit it, or does it belong to a spouse?) The answer to your question is yes, leaving the house (Cauda Draconis, the symbol of endings and departures) will be what you want it to be (Fortuna Minor in the first, springing to the 3rd). Amissio is favorable for any situation involving loss or letting go, and this is a question about letting go of a property, so I'm not sure why you see it as a negative figure. Quite the contrary -- look at the witnesses. The right witness suggests that you've had a long road to walk (in at least one sense) to get to this situation, and the left witness suggests that letting go of this property will enable you to get something better. The role of Tristitia simply suggests to me that you'll feel sad about leaving a familiar place.

I've been mulling over a seeming contradiction, and I'd like to know if I'm on the right track. It's to do with two things: the first is that you've said that we have to learn to be able to take on physical bodies, then etheric bodies, then astral bodies, then mental bodies, then spiritual bodies. The second is that all things start on the spiritual plane, and then descend down to the physical level, or stop before that.

What I've come up with is that everything that has a physical presence has an etheric, astral, mental, and spiritual presence, but that the bodies are the part of us which stops at each level. So the physical body stops at the physical plane, the etheric body isn't just the etheric support for the physical body but includes elements which stop there (thus why it can detach), same with the astral body, and the mental and spiritual bodies we will develop later.

Do you think this is on the right track, or do you think I'm making a mistake here somewhere?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You've basically got the right idea. The descent from spirit to matter is involution; the return from matter to spirit is evolution. Your soul, at the beginning of its incarnationary journey, starts out as part of a stream of formless soul-stuff that descends from spirit into matter -- that is, gradually focuses its awareness down from its normal free state into a state of intense focus on material being. Once all the way down in the world of matter, you start building bodies one at a time, starting gfrom matter, and those bodies are your ladder back up. So you leave spirit as a non-individualized part of a current, and return to spirit as a wholly individualized being capable of embodiment and action on every plane of being.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may -
The Michael Teaching has pretty much the same process: a large glob of completely integrated individualities decides to further evolve using the physical plane. The process of involution is one of progressive fragmentation: on each level, that glob fragments into more and more individual pieces. On the way down, it leaves some kind of a trace or breadcrumbs or whatever to guide it on the way back up.

The way back up is a process of successive reintegration. Michael, who exists on the Causal plane, describes themselves as a partially integrated composite entity of 1050 Astral fragments. (That corresponds to several hundred thousand Physical Plane lifetimes.)

I know a good deal more detail in this system, but as far as I'm concerned it's interesting rather than useful.

John Roth

This is similar to what Meher Baba states in God Speaks. On p. 176 he says:

"The soul has to pass through eight million, four hundred thousand (eighty-four lahks) human forms after it has once commenced to reincarnate in the human form until it has attained God-Realization. He writes that our physical universe actually contains gas-states that are so incredibly subtle that there are no human devices that are capable of detecting them and *eons* are spent in these gas-states (What Baba calls the first-most finite expression of the Infinite Nothingness's whim to 'know itself') these eventually begin to densify until they coalesce into various stone-forms, then the energies evolve to metal-forms, to plant-forms, to insect-forms, worms, fish, birds, animals of various forms and finally to human wherein the evolution of Consciousness (humans know that we know) is finally complete and ready for the next stage toward more subtle realms of power, wisdom and bliss.

So it is said that God created the universe in seven days. From the Nothing, most finite shadow and infinite shadow emerged simultaneously.

replies.title: Re: Banishing Ritual
replies.content:
1) Quite a few of them. You can find one very effective one, the Sphere of Protection, in posts on this Dreamwidth journal starting here.

3) The LBRP evolved out of a ritual called the Calling of the Four, which Eliphas Levi gives; where Levi got it is an interesting question. Before then occultists had to use somewhat clumsier methods of banishing, involving either incense and holy water or extensive prayers.

replies.title: Re: Banishing Ritual
replies.content:
Thank you. In looking over the Sphere of Protection, I thought it was an LBRP variation with different names for Deities. Appreciate you correcting me. Will look at it closer then.

Interesting background on banishing rituals. That explains why so much out there is a variation on the LBRP. Didn't realize what a major event the releasing of the Golden Dawn material was.

replies.title: Re: Banishing Ritual
replies.content: The publication of The Golden Dawn by Israel Regardie will be seen by historians of the occult as one of the most important events of the modern era. Before then magical rituals had by definition been kept secret by initiatory orders; from then on, the trickle of serious magical instruction into the public domain has gradually turned into a torrent. Those of us who want to practice magic are exceedingly lucky to be alive at such a time!

comments.title: Magic in a foreign language
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I plan to start an occult practice in a few years once my children grow up (yes, I've read the FAQ). However I am not a native English speaker, hence my questions:

Does it matter whether someone performs a ritual in a foreign language, or in their native language?

Generally speaking, how does one go about translating a ritual to another language?

Thanks,
faluc

replies.title: Re: Magic in a foreign language
replies.content: Rituals are translated all the time. The key is that you leave the names and words of power untranslated, but translate the rest. For example, if you're translating the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, you leave all the names and the Hebrew words in the Cabalistic Cross untranslated, but replace the English words with the language of your choice. (In Latin, for example, the calling of the archangels would begin "Ante me, Raphael, retro me, Gabriel," and so on.)

replies.title: Re: Magic in a foreign language
replies.content: If you're translating a ritual out of the language it's initially written in, how do you tell what the words of power are?

replies.title: Re: Magic in a foreign language
replies.content: The words of power are never in ordinary language. They're generally names of beings, and are either words in a sacred language or in an unknown one.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

1. I bought the Druidry Handbook to supplement my beginnings with the work as described in the DMH. In the Handbook, a technique for receptive meditation is included, which you describe is closer to certain Asian meditation practices. Is the main difference between say meditation in Buddhism and receptive meditation in Druidry that the focus in the latter is on listening closely to nature?

2. You described some time back the concept of ego inflation. Could I trouble you to elaborate what that means? Is it a danger to beginners in Druid magic?

Thanks as always!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes. The important thing is that you're not just emptying your mind -- you're filling it with something else -- in this case, your perceptions of nature.

2) Ego inflation is a danger to everyone, and it's especially risky for the novice mage. Basically, it's what happens when you convince yourself that you're bigger and stronger and more important than you are. Your ego inflates like a balloon -- thus the name -- and you talk yourself into believing that you're immensely powerful and the world has to do whatever you tell it. What makes this dangerous, of course, is that if you've had self-confidence problems, the early stages of ego inflation can actually improve things for you, because you have the confidence to succeed. The problem is that you end up turning into an insufferable jerk, and discover the hard way that overblown self-confidence will not get you what you want. The best way to avoid ego inflation is to remember that you are no more important, special, or powerful than any other beginning mage, and that the universe does not know that you're here and will not miss you when you're gone.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you!

2) One of the things that attracted me to Druidry in the first place is the emphasis it seems to place on balancing the self and the rest of the cosmos, which should (hopefully) prevent things such as ego inflation happening. Through a lot of self-reflection, I have discovered I have a weird combination of an overdeveloped sense of entitlement together with a deep lack of self-confidence. I know, it doesn't make sense, but I am trying to sort it out.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That combination isn't weird at all -- it's very common. People raised with an unrealistic sense of entitlement generally know at some level that they don't actually deserve what they think they ought to have, and so they end up with very poor self-confidence. The best way to overcome that is to develop strength of will, and use it to tackle things that are at least a little challenging, where there's a definite risk of failure. When you fail, you learn that failure isn't the end of the world; when you succeed, you learn that you're capable of doing things...and over time, that builds confidence and strength.

comments.content:
Not a question, but an update.

Some time ago, I asked about a part of my town that consistently flips my internal compass 180 degrees. Really messes with my sense of direction, and has for decades.

Since then, I've moved to the other side of town, and now drive that area much more frequently: and the flipping effect is gone. Not like a gradual getting used to the area type thing, but like one day, I drove through there, mentally preparing for it, so I wouldn't take a wrong turn, or get confused and turn around... and it didn't happen.

It was the first time I'd driven that route since the hurricane, and I can't say for sure why the effect is gone, but I have some theories:

2. The hurricane so dramatically changed the landscape that whatever visual cues I was getting hung up on are gone, and (like the rest of town since the storm), I've had to re-learn how to navigate it as though it were a whole new place.

3. The area's AM radio tower blew over in the storm, and the station was forced to relocate. I am sore tempted to find out where the station is operating from now, and go drive around that neighborhood, just to see what happens...

comments.title: Devotion to Archetypes
comments.content:
I'm wondering about prayer and devotional practices to an archetype as opposed to a god. I ask because an archetype has gotten my attention, and I've given it a place on my home altar. I'm able to communicate with it successfully, but the only name I have for it so far is the generic name of the archetype. I've recently asked it in prayer to make known to me a name by which I should call it, if there be such.

So this being and I are developing our relationship. What I'm wondering is if there are any special considerations when practicing devotion to an archetype as such. Is there precedent for this? What's your understanding of the differences and relationship between archetypes and deities?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Devotion to Archetypes
replies.content:
It's actually quite common to make offerings to deities of whom all you know is the basic archetypal form. In Japanese Shinto, for example, there are plenty of kami of whom absolutely nothing is known -- their name amounts to "the kami of such and such a place," and if you go to the shrine and make the appropriate offering, your rice crop does better, or your kids have a better chance in their exams, or what have you. In Roman times it was standard for local gods throughout the Empire to be assigned the label of the nearest approximate Roman god; if it's a warrior god, it's some form of Mars, and if the locals have a name or title for it that gets tacked on. (A Roman shrine dedicated to Batman, if such a thing existed, would doubtless have an altar inscribed to Mars Vespertilianus.)

So you're in line with an old tradition. As you work with the power in question, it'll tell you what titles it prefers and what offerings strengthen the bond between you. Follow that, and don't worry about theory.

replies.title: Re: Devotion to Archetypes
replies.content: Why "Vespertilianus"?

replies.title: Re: Devotion to Archetypes
replies.content: Latin for bat

replies.title: Re: Devotion to Archetypes
replies.content: Vespertilio = Latin for "bat" in the flying-cave-creature sense

replies.title: Re: Devotion to Archetypes
replies.content:
Exactly -- vespertilio is the Latin for "bat," and since Latin doesn't do many mashed-together words like Batman, Vespertilianus -- roughly, "he of the bat" -- would be the equivalent of Batman in classical Latin.

I once indulged myself in imagining a series of Batman adventures set in ancient Rome, with Bruce Wayne's distant ancestor putting on a black toga and a mask to fight crime as Vespertilianus, with his sidekick Passer, Puer Mirabilis -- passer is "sparrow" rather than "robin," but "robin" in Latin is turdus, and that has all the wrong connotations! Most of it, I suspect, was the sheer delight in imagining Adam West in a toga saying "Celerrime, Passer, ad speluncam verpertilianam!"

(Yes, that's "Quickly, Robin, to the Batcave!")

comments.title: Dreams per se
comments.content:
Last week Astral Projection was discussed a bit… I have never done that or attempted as far as I am aware, however I have attempted Lucid Dreaming via the Wake Induced Lucid Dreaming (WILD) method discussed by Stephen LaBerge… as opposed to the other method (which I can’t recall the acronym) of going to sleep as normal and simply recognizing (or testing, often by looking at one’s watch…) if you were dreaming and if so, becoming lucid (sometimes I could do this, but not consistently)… Although whether I was doing things properly and whether things were a dream or something else, I have never been sure of.

The specifics of the method I used are the following: I would lay in bed and kind of get into a meditative state, usually focusing on a mental image of a candle flame or some such… and then I would let myself drift off, while mentally throwing my feet and legs up in an attempt to float of the bed… once I could fly, I assumed I was dreaming. However I was still in my bedroom or at least that was the surrounding decorations.

This was an extremely exhilarating experience, although requires a good bit of effort… the one weird thing was looking into mirrors in this state… they were all… weird… and I could not see myself in the reflection (it was kind of blurred or washed out) But one time, while looking in my bathroom mirror in this presumed lucid state… a person leaned in (from inside the mirror) and waved and was kind of laughing like a friend messing with another friend might. I don’t know this person and didn’t recognize them, but it was a middle aged lady with longish hair… ( I am a middle-aged man with … pretty much no hair :)

Anyhow, it was weird!

Does this sound like lucid dreaming or was I accidentally or unknowingly doing something else? Looking to understand as to better understand what I was doing and whether or not I should resume.

(I resorted to the Wake Induced method as the training to “test” for a dream state using the other method was to build up a habit of doing checking if one is in a dream… by “testing” for a dream state during the normal day… questioning reality several times a day kind of started messing with my head a bit… so I opted for the Wake Induced method…)

replies.title: Re: Grave Matters
replies.content:
Some cultures expose the dead to the elements on a platform or on bare ground for the birds to scavenge. That might be considered burial by air. Some of the Native Americans on the Great Plains did so, constructing head high platforms, leaving the corpse, dressed in their best and with shield, medicine bag and weapons hanging from the poles. The Tibetans cut the body in pieces, grinding large bones to powder and let the birds come. The Zoroastrians construct flat topped towers that are hollow. Bodies are laid on top to be scavenged' any remaining bones are pushed through a hole to fall into the hollow tower.

The Zoroastrian custom is caused by a desire to avoid polluting either the earth or the fire, both of which are sacred. The Tibetan custom is a result of too little wood for cremation and soil frozen too hard for burial most of the year. The NA practice is also in an area with too little wood for cremation to be practical and probably lack of tools to dig a grave deep enough to shelter the body from predators such as wolves. The prairie tall grass had long, thick roots that would have made digging without a metal shovel very difficult, and nomadic peoples would not expect to return to tend a grave in any case. American pioneers would sometime drive horses or oxen over a grave to compact the soil enough that animals wouldn't dig up the remains.

There are people in the US trying for similar options. I like birds, so cool, but can't convince my adult children "Mom, we are _not_ feeding you to the vultures!!"

replies.title: Re: Grave Matters
replies.content:
1) Once the etheric body has separated entirely from the physical body -- a process that can take up to three days -- the physical body is compost, and can be dealt with in any desired method without any difference in its effect on the higher bodies. If you destroy the physical body before the etheric body has finished separating, that's kind of a jolt, but it's rarely a serious problem.

2) That's getting into rather creepy territory. In very ancient times, there was a well-developed method by which certain individuals could leave the cycle of incarnation for a time to serve as the guardian spirit of a family, a community, or a place. That's what the long barrows of northwest Europe were about -- one person every few generations would enter the mound, die in a carefully controlled fashion, and have their body preserved in some way, releasing the previous guardian and taking his or her place. Later, the tradition became corrupt, and wealthy and powerful people started using it to try to become immortal; that's what the pyramids were meant to do. Later still, the practice of embalming survived even though the meaning had trickled out of it; in still other place,s cremation or burial in earth (which leads to quick decay) became standard as a way to shut down a corrupt practice; in still other places, a debased and predatory version of the same practice survived, giving rise to the legends of vampirism. I talk about some of this in my book Monsters.

3) Arrange for your body to be left unburied and unembalmed for three days -- refrigeration is fine. Then have the survivors deal with it however you like. I plan on being cremated and having the ashes buried under a newly planted sapling.

replies.title: Re: Views of the Christian God
replies.content: Alfred North Whitehead used to say that a lot of traditional theology amounts to "metaphysical flattery," and I think he was quite correct. I see no reason to think that the Christian god created the universe; I see no reason to think that the universe was created. (It's a living thing, not an artifact.) To my mind, just as Christians are wrong to claim that their god is the only god there is, they're wrong to claim that their god created the universe. It's rather like the five-year-old who is convinced that his daddy is the biggest and strongest and best man who ever existed; factually, it's not accurate, but it's an expression of the child's overflowing love and admiration, and so the right thing to do is smile and nod and catch the father's smile and wink.

replies.title: Re: Shamanism & Druidry & Magic
replies.content: If you want to seek GCC ordination, you need to pursue the AODA path and not the DOGD. More generally, AODA is much more open to the kind of path you've described. The DOGD teaches a very specific, tightly focused system of Druidical ceremonial magic, and that's all -- it's not a mix-and-match kind of order. AODA, by contrast, is intentionally open to a very broad range of practices.

comments.title: Chage of location -> change of deities?!?!
comments.content:
I've had a disconcerting experience since moving into my current location (The Village Retirement Center, Gainesville, FL). I set up my altar as usual with Pan and the Primordial Mother, and sat down before them, and kept getting the feeling that they were ... not irrelevant, precisely, but would be replaced by the Goddess in a different form (still vague) and that Pan would disappear, to be replaced by the Horned One as a deer. The ones I had seemed to be about to fade into the sunset as if their day in their form had been limited to Albuquerque. Or at least the desert and the mountains of the West.

I have asked for guidance and actually started to relax after a banishing, relaxing, and 4-fold breathing before bedtime. And lat down to vague dream-images of being surrounded by old ladies whom I could not hear or who were unwilling to speak yet, and the whole thing seemed to be shades of pastels. (Note, I had been afraid of being suffocated in pastel gentility for sure, though they're a lot more lively than that here.) The relaxation went away rather quickly after that.

Do you have any explanation and recommendations?

Pat

replies.title: Re: Chage of location -> change of deities?!?!
replies.content: That's actually a very common experience in traditional Pagan societies. Your ancient Greek merchant who sailed to Egypt made offerings to Isis and Osiris, and if he then sailed to southern Gaul he'd make offerings to Celtic deities there. It was commonly held that the deities (at least the major ones) were in some sense the same beings, but they manifested in different forms in different parts of the world. Don't worry about it -- the deities are still with you, they're just changing their forms as you changed your location.

comments.title: History of Lesser Pentagram Ritual
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I am a long time reader of your work, both your books and your blogs, and I've worked with the preliminary materials of your Celtic Golden Dawn system. Thank you so much for all the fabulous work you do!

I am wondering if you know when the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram originated. Was it with Eliphas Levi? Did it predate him? Was it used in antiquity, or is it a 19th century creation? What is the first known form of this ritual, and who first used it?

Thanks a million,
Jonathan S.

replies.title: Re: History of Lesser Pentagram Ritual
replies.content: Nobody knows. Levi includes a discussion (carefully split up between several chapters) of a ritual called the Calling of the Four, which seems to be the immediate predecessor of the LRP. Nobody knows where Levi got it, and nobody knows when it turned into the LRP as we know it -- all we know is that when the Golden Dawn got started in 1887, the LRP was in existence.

replies.title: Re: History of Lesser Pentagram Ritual
replies.content: I find this happens quite a bit with certain spiritual practices. At first I was inclined to think it's secrecy, but then I began to wonder if there's something more to it. You mentioned before thinking certain rituals get their energies from people casting them in the future, so it got me thinking: what if people are able to "tune in", so to speak, and it came into existence because people tapped into something on the astral plane?

replies.title: Re: History of Lesser Pentagram Ritual
replies.content: That's certainly a possibility, too. At the same time, there was a very intense culture of secrecy back in the day. It hadn't been all that long, all things considered, since being caught practicing ceremonial magic meant that you risked a really horrible death...
